title: Ta Prohm - the Buddhist Monastery - Cambodia Travel-Magazine
highlights: Prohm was built at the end of the 12th century (in 1186) during the reign of King Jayavarman VII. Dedicated to his royal mother Prajnaparamita, a statue that resembles her is placed in the central tower of the temple. Ta Prohm temple has a total of 260 statues of Buddha. On the west gate are carvings of Garuda which were made in the same style like the ones on the temple of Preah Khan and Banteay Kdey. In ancient times 18 chief monks, 600 dancing  ...    It is the only temple that has not been restored and has been left to the power of nature by french archaeologists who discovered the forgotten temple in the Angkor area. title: Exploring Royal-Familial Visual Configurations in Cambodia
highlights: making, enshrining, and dedication of statues within temple complexes. Ritual dedication via honorific titles was a key aspect of Angkorian semiotics of power and thus the names of a king and his  ...    However, the cult of expressing kingship appears to have altered little. Jayavarman VII employed the same mechanisms of  ...    Thus, he continued in the tradition of consecrating temples and statues to his ancestors utilizing honorific and divine titles, but he did so on an unprecedented scale and using a clear triadic model.[[28]]He dedicated the vast temple of Preah Khan to his father, apotheosised as the bodhisattva Lokeśvara, and the temple of Ta Prohm to his mother, apotheosised as the bodhisattva Prajnāpāramitā. The result of the union of the (male) bodhisattva of compassion and the (female) bodhisattva of wisdom is the Buddha.
